title: Why your site is broken on a phone
testCase: Is there a <meta name="viewport"> in the <head>?
blocks.0.text: The page is flawless. Semantic, clean, every tag in place. On a computer it looks exactly right.
blocks.1.text: Open it on a phone. Everything is tiny. The letters are the size of ants. You have to pinch-zoom to read anything at all.
blocks.2.text: You did nothing wrong. And still your site is useless to most of the people who will open it.
blocks.3.text: The phone is lying to you
blocks.4.text: Here is what happens, and it sounds absurd the first time you hear it.
blocks.5.text: The phone is maybe 400 pixels wide. But it does not tell the page that. It says: "I am 980 pixels wide." It lies on purpose.
blocks.6.text: Then it builds the page for a width of 980 — and SHRINKS the whole thing to fit the screen. Like looking at a newspaper from three metres away.
blocks.7.text: Why it does this at all
blocks.8.text: Remember the table lesson? For twenty years sites were built with tables, for desktop screens. When the iPhone appeared, those sites numbered in the millions — and not one was built for a phone.
blocks.9.text: Shown honestly, at 400 pixels, every one of them would have broken. So the phone lied: "I am as wide as a computer." It shrank the page and showed all of it, tiny, but whole.
blocks.10.text: That was the right call — then. And it has stayed the default ever since. Your page pays the price for sins committed twenty years ago.
blocks.13.items.0: width=device-width — "be as wide as you REALLY are". No more lying.
blocks.13.items.1: initial-scale=1 — "and do not shrink anything. One pixel is one pixel."
blocks.14.text: One line. In the <head>, with the other meta tags. And the phone stops pretending.
blocks.17.text: Do not get confused. This line does not make your site responsive. It only stops the phone from lying.
blocks.18.text: The real work — the menu collapsing into a hamburger, columns stacking, images scaling down — that is CSS and a whole course in itself.
blocks.19.text: But without this one line, none of the CSS will work. You will write rules for 400 pixels while the phone thinks it is 980. That is why it is here — it is the door.
blocks.20.text: Your turn. Tell the phone the truth.
